coredata.dc:title: Optical and luminescence properties of carbon dot-doped sulphamic acid crystals: A potential candidate for LED and display technologies
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 The Author(s). Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Author(s). Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.dc:creator: Akshata, B.R.
Deepthi, P.R.
Mohankumar, P.
Sukhdev, Anu
Challa, Malathi
coredata.dc:description: This study is the first to investigate the influence of carbon dots (CD) on the optical and luminescence properties of sulphamic acid (SA) crystals, with the aim of exploring their potential in optical device applications. High-quality single crystals of pure SA and CD-doped variants (5NCSA and 10NCSA) were synthesized using the slow evaporation solution growth method. The crystalline quality and structural properties were evaluated through powder X-ray diffraction (XRD) analysis. The Williamson–Hall (W–H) method was employed to calculate crystallite size and analyze the anisotropic strain effects introduced by CD doping, with findings compared to those from the Monshi-Scherrer method. Functional group analysis was conducted using FTIR spectroscopy, while SEM coupled with EDAX provided insights into surface morphology and confirmed CD incorporation. Optical transmission studies evaluated transparency in the UV–visible spectrum. The measured band gap values were 5.86 eV for pure SA, 5.77 eV for 5NCSA, and 5.55 eV for 10NCSA. Furthermore, Raman analysis confirmed the presence of CD within the SA lattice. The photoluminescence (PL) spectra were recorded to analyze emissions at specific excitation wavelengths. PL measurements showed emissions at 373 and 425 nm for pure SA, and at 374 and 426 nm for doped variants, with Commission Internationale de l’Eclairage (CIE) coordinates in the intense blue region. Notably, 10NCSA demonstrated enhanced fluorescence under UV light, surpassing that of pure SA. These findings highlight the potential of CD-doped SA crystals for applications in LED technology, display systems, and fluorescent boards.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon Dot-doped crystals
X-ray diffraction analysis
Optical properties
Scanning electron microscopy
Photoluminescence
Fluorescence

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Photograph of the as grown crystals and (b) schematic diagram of the synthesis process.
(a) Powder X-ray diffraction pattern of pure SA, 5NCSA and 10NCSA crystals and (b) peak shift.
W–H plot of SA, 5NCSA and 10NCSA crystals.
Monshi-Scherrer plots of SA, 5NCSA and 10NCSA crystals.
FTIR spectra of SA, 5NCSA and 10NCSA crystals.
Raman spectra of SA, 5NCSA and 10NCSA crystals.
(a) UV–Vis–NIR transmittance spectra of SA, 5NCSA and 10NCSA crystals. (b) (αhυ)2 versus hυ spectra of SA, 5NCSA and 10NCSA crystals.
(a) SEM Images of pure SA and (b) 10NCSA.
(a) EDAX Images of pure SA and (b) 10NCSA.
Photoluminescence spectra of SA, 5NCSA and 10NCSA crystals.
(a) CIE spectra of SA, 5NCSA and 10NCSA crystals and b) the representation of energy transition.
Photograph represents the fluorescence properties under UV light for (a) SA, (b) 5NCSA and (c) 10NCSA crystals.
Crystallite size and strain for SA, 5NCSA and 10NCSA crystals.

head.ce:title: Optical and luminescence properties of carbon dot-doped sulphamic acid crystals: A potential candidate for LED and display technologies

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Physics, Presidency University, Bengaluru, Karnataka, India
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Physics, Presidency University, Bengaluru, Karnataka, India.

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Material Research Centre, Presidency University, Bengaluru, Karnataka, India
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Material Research Centre, Presidency University, Bengaluru, Karnataka, India.

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Presidency University, Bengaluru, Karnataka, India
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, Presidency University, Bengaluru, Karnataka, India.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Chemistry, Ramaiah Institute of Technology

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: Department of Physics, Presidency University, Bengaluru, Karnataka, India.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: This study is the first to investigate the influence of carbon dots (CD) on the optical and luminescence properties of sulphamic acid (SA) crystals, with the aim of exploring their potential in optical device applications. High-quality single crystals of pure SA and CD-doped variants (5NCSA and 10NCSA) were synthesized using the slow evaporation solution growth method. The crystalline quality and structural properties were evaluated through powder X-ray diffraction (XRD) analysis. The Williamson–Hall (W–H) method was employed to calculate crystallite size and analyze the anisotropic strain effects introduced by CD doping, with findings compared to those from the Monshi-Scherrer method. Functional group analysis was conducted using FTIR spectroscopy, while SEM coupled with EDAX provided insights into surface morphology and confirmed CD incorporation. Optical transmission studies evaluated transparency in the UV–visible spectrum. The measured band gap values were 5.86 eV for pure SA, 5.77 eV for 5NCSA, and 5.55 eV for 10NCSA. Furthermore, Raman analysis confirmed the presence of CD within the SA lattice. The photoluminescence (PL) spectra were recorded to analyze emissions at specific excitation wavelengths. PL measurements showed emissions at 373 and 425 nm for pure SA, and at 374 and 426 nm for doped variants, with Commission Internationale de l’Eclairage (CIE) coordinates in the intense blue region. Notably, 10NCSA demonstrated enhanced fluorescence under UV light, surpassing that of pure SA. These findings highlight the potential of CD-doped SA crystals for applications in LED technology, display systems, and fluorescent boards.

ce:section.ce:para: The development of efficient luminescent nonlinear optical (NLO) materials has garnered significant global interest in optoelectronic device research. Bulk single crystals, in particular, hold high value for their versatile applications in optical communication, image processing, photonics and optoelectronics. Compared to organic NLO materials, inorganic crystals offer notable advantages, such as enhanced thermal and mechanical stability, making them particularly suited for applications in optical switching, laser systems, holographic data storage, harmonic generation, optical computing, colour displays medical diagnostics and information processing. Incorporating dopants is essential for improving the performance of these single crystals, as they play a significant role in optimizing growth rates and enhancing functional properties [1,2]. By introducing specific dopants, researchers can tailor the physical attributes of these crystals, thus creating innovative materials for advanced optoelectronic applications [3].

Recently, there has been a growing interest among researchers in carbon dots (CDs) due to their efficient adsorption properties, enhanced biocompatibility, surface area-to-volume ratio, band gap and morphology [4–7]. CD’s are recognized for their tunable emission, low toxicity, high solubility, luminescence properties and photo stability, positioning them as a promising new generation alternative to heavy metal incorporated quantum dots. Their distinctive characteristics have led to significant interest in applications such as photocatalysis, nano-sensors, biomedical uses, bio-imaging and optical sensing [8]. Moreover, CD's feature various hydroxyl groups on their surface that can readily produce hydroxyl radicals upon light irradiation [9]. This generation of highly reactive intermediates facilitates the photocatalytic oxidation of dye molecules by converting solar energy into chemical energy [10]

Sulphamic acid (H₂NSO₃), the monoamide derivative of sulfuric acid, is a potent inorganic acid known for its zwitterionic properties when dissolved in deionized water. The presence of a zwitterionic structure enhances the crystal hardness. Additionally, sulphamate derivatives, with their unique two planar ring configuration, exhibit superior blue light transmittance and remarkable optical nonlinearity, distinguishing them among inorganic materials [11]. There is currently significant interest in developing new materials for various applications, with a particular focus on the growth of pure and doped crystals of substantial size.

Numerous studies have explored the growth and analysis of sulphamic acid (SA) crystals with various amino acid dopants [12]. Enhanced nonlinear optical (NLO) properties when doped with alkali metals was reported in literature [13,14]. Several efforts have focused on synthesizing SA crystals doped with metal ions [15–18].Various scientists investigated the impact of sodium and potassium chloride on SA crystals [19,20]. Few dye doped SA crystals were reported for optoelectronic applications [3]. FeSO4's effects on the thermal and structural characteristics of sulphamic acid single crystals have been documented in the literature [21]. Crystals doped with carbon dots have not received much attention in the literature. The application of carbon dot-doped NaCl crystals for Fabry-Perot lasing and white-light generation has been investigated by Prakash et al. [22]. There are currently no reports on carbon dot-doped sulphamic acid (SA) single crystals in the literature. In light of this, we present a comprehensive study on the growth, optical, and luminescence properties of SA crystals doped with neem-derived carbon dots. This study is the first to investigate the incorporation of carbon dots into sulphamic acid (SA) single crystals, addressing a gap in the literature. While previous studies have explored amino acid, alkali metal, and metal ion doping, this work introduces a novel approach using neem-derived carbon dots, unlocking new optical and luminescence properties.

This study investigates the impact of neem-derived carbon dots (CDs) on the growth and crystalline habit of sulphamic acid (SA) crystals. The slow evaporation approach was used to generate single crystals of neem carbon dot-doped sulphamic acid (NCSA). For the first time, a systematic analysis was conducted to explore the physical properties of these crystals, including their structural characteristics, UV–visible absorption, optical band gap, surface morphology, elemental composition (EDAX), and photoluminescence behaviour.

Commercial sulphamic acid (Merck, ≥99.5 %, AR grade) served as the starting material for crystal growth. Twelve grams of sulphamic acid were dissolved in 50 mL of distilled water and continuously stirred with a magnetic stirrer for three hours to achieve a homogeneous solution. The saturated solution was then filtered through Whatman filter paper and set aside to evaporate in a dust-free environment. Clear, colourless, and well-defined crystals up to 28 × 20 × 16 mm³, in size were produced after four weeks [22].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Growth of carbon dot doped SA
ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots were synthesized through a green synthesis method using freshly plucked neem leaves (Azadirachta indica). The leaves were thoroughly washed and ground into a fine paste. Six grams of this paste were mixed with 60 mL of double-distilled water and transferred to a stainless steel autoclave. For four hours, the autoclave was heated to 160°C in a hot air oven. Afterward, the resulting dark brown solution was filtered through Whatman filter paper and stored at room temperature.

A saturated solution of sulphamic acid was prepared, to which 5 mL or 10 mL of the synthesized neem carbon dot solution was separately added. To achieve homogeneity, the mixture was vigorously agitated for three hours. After that, the solution was filtered and allowed to evaporate in a dust-free atmosphere. Light brown crystals were extracted in great condition after 9 weeks, with a maximum size of 23 × 18 × 15 mm³ for 5NCSA and 39 × 40 × 19 mm³ for 10NCSA. Photographs of the formed crystals are shown in Fig. 1(a), and a schematic model of the synthesis process is shown in Fig. 1(b).

Since XRD and FTIR are characterisation techniques that are typically performed once on representative samples, the tests were performed once for each sample in the research. To guarantee data dependability, the measurements were made properly and under closely monitored circumstances. To ensure reproducibility, all spectroscopic investigations, including photoluminescence (PL), fluorescence analyses, and UV–visible measurements, were performed in triplicate (n = 3).

XRD analysis was performed using an analytical X-ray diffractometer with a CuKα source of radiation (λ = 1. 54 Å) to assess the crystalline structure of the samples. The XRD patterns were obtained for powdered samples of pure SA, 5NCSA, and 10NCSA, within the 2θ range of 15° to 60°. As shown in Fig. 2(a), the incorporation of carbon dots led to modifications in the positions of peak towards higher angles and subtle changes in peak intensities. These shifts (Fig. 2(b)) are influenced by the dopant size, which affects the lattice structure and d-spacing. When a dopant smaller than the host material fills the lattice sites, it alters the crystal structure. The existence of distinct Bragg peaks further confirms the high crystallinity of the grown crystals. The XRD pattern of pure SA closely aligns with patterns (JCPDS file no: 08–0483) reported in the literature [14,20,23,24]

The synthesised samples' lattice strain and crystallite size were ascertained using the Williamson-Hall (W-H) method. The W-H formula is as follows [25].
(1)
β cos θ =
K λ
D
+ 4 η sin θ
where η represents the lattice strain. The W-H graphs are shown in the Fig. 3. The slope of the W-H plot provides the strain, while the intercept corresponds to the crystallite size. The calculated strain for pure SA aligns with values reported in the literature [23,26]. The internal strain in the doped crystals is greater than in pure SA, as Table 1 illustrates. This increase in strain is due to the local deformation around the SA molecules as the unit cells stretch to accommodate the dopant. The variability in unit cell sizes generates a net internal strain within the crystals [3]. The negative strain (negative slope) observed in the Williamson-Hall (W-H) plot suggests a compressive lattice strain in the crystal structure. This happens when the microstrain in the material leads to a contraction of the lattice rather than an expansion. A comparable effect has been reported in the literature for SA crystals [3,26].

The second method for estimating D is the Monshi-Scherrer (M-S) method. This technique enhances the accuracy of D estimation by incorporating a logarithmic function [25]. The D values obtained using the M-S method exhibit a behavior closely resembling that of the W-H method. The results of the M-S method are shown in Table 1 and Fig. 4.

The FTIR spectra of SA, 5NCSA and 10NCSA crystals were obtained using a Perkin–Elmer spectrophotometer with the KBr pellet method, covering the wavenumber range of 400–4000 cm⁻¹, as illustrated in Fig. 5. The peaks detected at 2878 cm⁻¹ correspond to the
NH
3
+
stretching vibrations, confirming the zwitterionic nature and hydrogen bonding in the SA crystal. The 2300–2600 cm⁻¹ assortment of vibrational bands is ascribed to N–H stretching. For the pure and doped crystals, the
NH
3
+
deformation frequencies appear at 1444 cm⁻¹ and 1432 cm⁻¹, while the
SO
3
−
stretching mode's degeneracy is observed at 1248 cm⁻¹. The peak at 1063 cm⁻¹ is associated with symmetric SO₃ deformation, and the rocking modes of
NH
3
+
are indicated by bands at 994 cm⁻¹, 1006 cm⁻¹ and 1005 cm⁻¹ for SA and NCSA, respectively, further validating the zwitterionic nature of the SA crystal[27]. Peaks at 682 cm⁻¹ and 681 cm⁻¹ arise from the SO₃ rocking mode, while the SO₃ deformation mode is linked to the peak at 540 cm⁻¹. The 5NCSA and 10 NCSA spectra shows slight shifts in peak positions and intensities, indicating the integration of carbon dot into the sulphamic acid crystal. The results for pure SA aligning well with existing literature [27–29]. The incorporation of N-CQD into SA was confirmed through FTIR analysis. A band at 3417 cm⁻¹ and 3428 cm⁻¹ in the doped samples were ascribed to O−H stretching. The presence of CO stretching was indicated by a band at 1788 cm⁻¹, and N−H bending was associated with a peak at 1650 cm⁻¹. Additionally, the peak at 1259 cm⁻¹ and 1248 cm⁻¹ suggested C−O/C−N stretching. These findings confirm the existence of functional groups such as −OH, −NH₂, and CO [30].
Fig. 6 presents the Raman spectra of the grown crystals. A prominent peak at 1062 cm⁻¹ appears in all spectra, likely corresponding to the SO₃ symmetric stretching vibration in sulphamic acid.
The SO₃ rocking and deformation modes are observed at 363 cm⁻¹ and 542 cm⁻¹, respectively.

The NH₃⁺ stretching vibration shifts from 3110 cm⁻¹ (SA) to 3121 cm⁻¹ (5NCSA) and further to 3132 cm⁻¹ (10NCSA), suggesting modifications in hydrogen bonding due to CD incorporation. The peak near 1280 cm⁻¹, 1572 cm−1 (5NCSA) and 1277 cm⁻¹ and 1549 cm−1 (10NCSA) might indicate interaction between CD's and sulphamate groups, altering vibrational characteristics. The presence of the D band and G band confirms the successful incorporation of carbon dots (CD) into sulphamic acid (SA). Our findings align well with previously reported literature [31,32].

Fig. 7(a) shows the optical transmittance spectra of pure SA, 5NCSA, and 10NCSA crystals that were taken between 200 and 800 nm in wavelength. The optical properties of these materials are crucial for assessing their suitability in optoelectronic applications. For many optical applications, crystals with shorter cut-off wavelengths and better transmittance, particularly in the 200–400 nm range, are frequently favoured [3]. As illustrated in Fig. 6(a), the SA single crystal shows a transmittance of approximately 87 %, indicating a broad optical transparency across the visible spectrum. Owing to its low cut-off wavelength (221 nm) and wide transparency in the UV-Visible range, SA is a strong candidate for electro-optical applications. However, 5NCSA and 10NCSA have substantially poorer transmittances than SA, which may be related to dopant molecules' adherence to the SA lattice. In the case of 5NCSA and 10NCSA, the reduction in transmittance could result from the strong absorption characteristics of the carbon dots, which interact with the light and reduce overall transparency. Similar results for dye-doped SA crystals have been observed in the literature [3]

The optical energy gap for the synthesized crystals was determined using the Tauc relation [33]
(2)
(
α h ϑ )
n
= A ( h ϑ −
E
g
)
where h is Planck’s constant, α is the absorption coefficient, ν represents the incident frequency, A is a constant, and Eg is the energy band gap. For indirect transitions, the exponent is 1/2, while for direct transitions, the exponent is 2. In the present case, it emerged that n = 2 was more suitable for the grown crystals. The pure SA crystal exhibited an energy gap of 5.86 eV, while the 5NCSA and 10 NCSA crystals showed a slightly reduced direct band gap of 5.77 eV and 5.55 eV respectively, as shown in Fig. 7(b). Our findings align with previously reported studies in the literature [20,23]. The interaction of the carbon dot molecules with the SA lattice introduces additional scattering centers and crystal defects, which lowers the transmission of NCSA crystals. This interaction also generates more localized states within the band gap, contributing to the reduced band gap compared to the pure material [27–29]. The large band gap values of the grown samples suggest their potential suitability for optoelectronic applications like UV optoelectronic devices, deep-UV photodetectors, and blue-emitting LEDs [22,23,34].

The effect of dopants on the surface morphology of the SA and 10NCSA crystals were examined using scanning electron microscopy, as shown in Fig. 8(a) and (b). The findings demonstrate that the atomic layers vary in size and appearance. The surface is free of cracks and has a fine morphology with microcrystals. The micrographs reveal that the crystals have formed agglomerated clusters with noticeable voids. These clusters exhibit irregular shapes and sizes, likely due to uneven temperature distribution during the synthesis process, which led to variations in crystal growth. Agglomerated cluster-like crystals have also been observed in the literature for Tb³ ⁺ and KCL doped SA crystals [28,35].

The chemical composition of the grown pure SA and 10NCSA crystals was determined using energy dispersive X-ray (EDAX) analysis. The results confirmed the incorporation of carbon dots in the SA crystals. The EDAX spectra, displayed in Fig. 9(a) and (b), revealed characteristic peaks indicating the presence of sulfur, nitrogen, oxygen, and carbon.

Photoluminescence (PL) spectroscopy is a powerful technique for providing direct insights into the molecular-level properties of materials, including shallow and deep defects as well as band gap states [36]. The PL spectrum of the sulfamic acid (SA) single crystal, as shown in Fig. 10, reveals emission bands at 374, 425, and 552 nm. The 374 nm peak likely corresponds to π-π* transitions, while the 425 nm peak is attributed to indirect electronic transitions within the crystal structure. The 552 nm emission is likely due to defect states or localized transitions caused by structural imperfections in the crystal lattice. These emissions highlight the complex interactions between electronic states and defects within the SA crystal [20]. Upon the addition of carbon dots to sulphamic acid, the emission band exhibited a slight shift to a higher wavelength, specifically around 426 nm, indicating a blue emission. The calculated energy for the prominent 425 nm peak is 2.92 eV, which falls within the blue light region [37]. Previous studies have reported strong blue shifts in the PL of sulfur-doped quantum carbon dots, with a maximum peak around 400–425 nm [38]. In sulphamic acid crystals, factors like exciton binding energy, defect states, or impurities can cause the PL band gap to appear smaller than the UV-Vis band gap because recombination can occur through these intermediate states rather than directly across the fundamental band gap. The increase in PL peak intensity in 5NCSA and 10NCSA is primarily due to the introduction of CDs, which provide new radiative recombination centers, enhance quantum confinement effects, introduce surface state emissions, and facilitate efficient energy transfer. These factors collectively boost the luminescence efficiency, making the doped samples more optically active than pure SA.

The dominant emission peak's colour coordinates were determined by converting it into the Commission Internationale de l’Eclairage (CIE) diagram. The CIE system was employed to analyse the emission peak intensity of the synthesized crystals, correlating it with the colour coordinates (x, y). Fig. 11 (a) demonstrates that carbon dot doping in sulphamic acid crystals causes a slight shift in emission colour as the doping concentration increases. This shift in emission wavelength is likely due to the interaction between carbon dots and the host crystal, altering the electronic structure and consequently the photoluminescence properties. Fig. 11 (b) represents the energy transition. The observed colour variations indicate that carbon dot doping can be employed to fine-tune the emission characteristics of the crystals for applications such as LEDs. The study suggests that the carbon dot-doped SA single crystal has potential as a material for blue light-emitting LEDs. Notably, carbon dot-doped NaCl crystals have been reported in the literature for white light generation [22].

The fluorescence properties of SA, 5NCSA and 10 NCSA under UV light are shown in Fig. 12 (a-c). Upon exposure to UV light, the pure SA crystal exhibited no fluorescence, while the 5NCSA crystal emitted a faint bluish-violet fluorescence, aligning with its inherent emission properties. In contrast, the 10NCSA crystal demonstrated significantly enhanced fluorescence. This increase is attributed to the carbon dot doping, which introduced additional fluorescent centers, resulting in a stronger and more vibrant emission. The 10 NCSA crystal appeared brighter and exhibited a more intense fluorescence under UV light compared to the pure SA, highlighting its potential for LED applications.

Despite being innovative, this study has some limitations that should be taken into account. When neem-derived carbon dots (CDs) are used, a degree of material specificity is introduced, which means that without further verification, the effects of doping might not be immediately applicable to CDs made from other sources. Furthermore, the results are directly related to the slow evaporation solution growth method of crystal synthesis; other methods, like temperature gradient crystallisation, can result in different defect profiles in SA crystals, which could affect the results. Additionally, the study was restricted to two doping concentrations (5NCSA and 10NCSA), which limits our comprehension of how different CD levels impact the optical and structural properties of the material. Finally, the study did not investigate the doped crystals' environmental and thermal stability, which are critical aspects of assessing their long-term dependability and performance in real-world optical device applications.

This study employed the slow evaporation solution growth method to synthesize high-quality single crystals of SA, 5NCSA, and 10NCSA. The crystals underwent various characterization techniques to assess their optical properties for potential device applications. Powder XRD and UV–vis–NIR spectroscopy confirmed the incorporation of dopants into the SA lattice. The powder XRD patterns of 5NCSA and 10NCSA showed no additional peaks, indicating minimal impact from the low dopant levels, with only slight changes in peak intensity and position. FTIR spectroscopy identified the presence of functional groups, while UV–vis–NIR analysis demonstrated that doping reduced both UV transparency and the energy band gap, which measured 5.86 eV for pure SA, 5.77 eV for 5NCSA, and 5.55 eV for 10NCSA. Raman and EDAX analysis confirmed the presence of carbon ions in the SA matrix. Photoluminescence (PL) spectra revealed emissions at 373 and 425 nm for pure SA, and at 374 and 426 nm for the doped crystals, with CIE coordinates corresponding to a vivid blue region. Under UV light, 10NCSA exhibited brighter and more intense fluorescence compared to pure SA, suggesting its suitability for applications in LED technology, display systems, and fluorescent boards. These findings indicate that carbon dot-doped SA crystals are promising candidates for optoelectronic devices.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors gratefully acknowledge the financial support granted via the University seed grant (File No: RI&C/Funded Project/SI-3 & 4 dated 08/11/2021) and thank the management of Presidency University, Bangalore.
